mainTopic: (continues) הבדל בין לוקח למוכר · אונאה בבעל הבית · אונאה לתגר והדרתן
overview: (1) Rav Nachman rules that a seller may return a sale indefinitely, unlike a buyer, based on the logic that the seller does not know the true value until he attempts to resell. (2) Cases are brought where Rav Chisda rules that there is no Ona'ah when buying from a Baal HaBayit, distinguishing between a merchant and a layman. (3) The Mishnah states there is Ona'ah for both buyer and seller and for merchants; the Gemara derives this from the Torah and discusses the opinions of R' Yehuda and R' Nathan regarding the right of return. (4) A dispute between Rav and Shmuel regarding the validity of a stipulation waiving Ona'ah, analyzed through the lens of R' Meir and R' Yehuda's views on conditions in monetary matters. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: Rav Nachman rules that a seller may return a sale indefinitely, unlike a buyer, based on the logic that the seller does not know the true value until he attempts to resell.
Cases are brought where Rav Chisda rules that there is no Ona'ah when buying from a Baal HaBayit, distinguishing between a merchant and a layman.

The Mishnah states there is Ona'ah for both buyer and seller and for merchants; the Gemara derives this from the Torah and discusses the opinions of R' Yehuda and R' Nathan regarding the right of return.

A dispute between Rav and Shmuel regarding the validity of a stipulation waiving Ona'ah, analyzed through the lens of R' Meir and R' Yehuda's views on conditions in monetary matters. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: תַּגָּרֵי לוֹד לָא שְׁכִיחַ דְּטָעוּ
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the difficulty by stating that the merchants of Lod rarely make price errors, so they preferred the Rabbis' view to limit the buyer's time, even if it limited their own.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara provides a resolution to the difficulty raised in the previous step. This is a setup line introducing the explanation.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara explains that the merchants of Lod rarely err in pricing. Therefore, they did not need the indefinite right to retract. They preferred the Rabbis' view because it limited the buyer's time to retract, which was a greater concern for them than their own rare need to retract.

keyTerms.meaning: it is not found that they err

phrases.english: The merchants of Lod are not found to err.

steps.title: The landlord of רָמֵי בַּר חָמָא sold a donkey and erred
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara relates a story about the landlord of רָמֵי בַּר חָמָא who sold a donkey and erred in the price, and sought a ruling from רַב נַחְמָן.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara presents a practical case to illustrate רַב נַחְמָן's ruling. This is a setup line introducing a narrative example.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The landlord of רָמֵי בַּר חָמָא sold a donkey and realized he had erred in the price. He was sad because he had waited longer than the time allowed for a buyer to retract. רָמֵי בַּר חָמָא sent him to רַב נַחְמָן, who ruled that a seller may always retract, allowing the landlord to return the sale.

keyTerms.meaning: he sent him before רַב נַחְמָן

phrases.aramaic: אוּשְׁפִּיזְכָנֵיהּ דְּרָמֵי בַּר חָמָא זַבֵּין חֲמָרָא וּטְעָה.
phrases.english: The landlord of Rami bar Ḥama sold a donkey and erred.

phrases.aramaic: אַשְׁכְּחֵיהּ דַּהֲוָה עֲצִיב, אֲמַר לֵיהּ: אַמַּאי עֲצִיבַתְּ?
phrases.english: He found him that he was sad, he said to him: Why are you sad?
He said to him: I sold a donkey and I erred.

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לֵיהּ: זִיל הֲדַר בָּךְ. אָמַר לֵיהּ:
phrases.english: He said to him: Go and renege on it. He said to him:

phrases.aramaic: הָא שְׁהַאי לִי יוֹתֵר מִכְּדֵי שֶׁאַרְאֶה
phrases.english: This has waited for me more than the time of showing
to a merchant or to my relative!
He sent him before Rav Naḥman,

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לֵיהּ לֹא שָׁנוּ אֶלָּא לוֹקֵחַ,
phrases.english: He said to him: They did not teach only for a buyer,
but a seller – forever returns.

steps.triggerLanguage: אוּשְׁפִּיזְכָנֵיהּ דְּרָמֵי בַּר חָמָא זַבֵּן חֲמָרָא וּטְעָה. אַשְׁכְּחֵיהּ דַּהֲוָה עֲצִיב, אֲמַר לֵיהּ: אַמַּי עֲצִיבַתְּ? אֲמַר לֵיהּ: זַבֵּינִי חֲמָרָא וּטְעַאי. אֲמַר לֵיהּ: זִיל הָדַר בָּךְ. אָמַר לֵיהּ: הָא שְׁהֵי לִי יוֹתֵר מִכְּדֵי שֶׁאֶרְאֶה לַתַּגָּר אוֹ לִקְרוֹבִי! שַׁדְרֵיהּ לְקַמֵּיהּ דְּרַב נַחְמָן, אֲמַר לֵיהּ לֹא שָׁנוּ אֶלָּא לוֹקֵחַ, אֲבָל מוֹכֵר – לְעוֹלָם חוֹזֵר.

steps.title: What is the reason for this ruling?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks for the underlying reason why a seller may retract indefinitely while a buyer may not.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara asks for the rationale behind רַב נַחְמָן's ruling. This is a setup line introducing the logical inquiry.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara seeks to understand the logic that distinguishes between the buyer and the seller. This question sets up the explanation that follows.

phrases.english: What is the reason? A buyer has his purchase in his hand,

phrases.aramaic: כֹּל הֵיכָא דְּאָזֵיל מַחְוִי לֵיהּ וְאָמְרִי
phrases.english: Wherever he goes, he shows it to them and they say
to him if he erred,
if he did not err.

phrases.aramaic: מוֹכֵר דְּלָא נָקֵט מִקָּחֵיהּ בִּידֵיהּ עַד
phrases.english: A seller who did not take his purchase in his hand until
that merchandise like his merchandise comes before him,

phrases.aramaic: וְיָדַע אִי טְעָה וְאִי לָא טְעָה.
phrases.english: and he knows if he erred and if he did not err.

steps.title: The case of the silk strips and רַבָּא's ruling
steps.stepSummary: A case is presented where a buyer tries to exploit the rules of אונאה by overpaying a non-merchant seller, and רַבָּא rules that אונאה does not apply to a homeowner.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with a narrative case involving silk strips, setting up a legal question about אונאה. This is a setup line introducing a new scenario.
steps.deeperAnalysis: A seller announces a price of six for an item worth five, but would accept five and a half. A buyer deliberately pays six to avoid the appearance of a waiver and instead claims אונאה in court. רַבָּא rules that the laws of אונאה only apply when buying from a merchant, not from a homeowner. This establishes the baseline ruling that the rest of the sugya will explore.

keyTerms.meaning: waiver / extraction of the right to claim

phrases.aramaic: הָהוּא גַּבְרָא דַּהֲוָה נָקֵט וַרְשְׁכֵי לְזַבּוֹנֵי,
phrases.english: That man who was holding silk strips to sell,
called out six and valued five,

phrases.aramaic: וְאִי הֲווֹ יָהֲבִי לֵיהּ חַמְשָׁא וּפַלְגָא הֲוָה שָׁקֵיל.
phrases.english: and if they gave him five and a half he would take.
That man came and said:

phrases.aramaic: אִי יָהֵיבְנָא לֵיהּ חַמְשָׁא וּפַלְגָא הָוְיָא מְחִילָה.
phrases.english: If I give him five and a half I would waive.
I will give him six and sue him in court.
He came before Rava, he said to him:

phrases.aramaic: לֹא שָׁנוּ אֶלָּא בְּלוֹקֵחַ מִן הַתַּגָּר,
phrases.english: They only taught regarding a buyer from a merchant,
but regarding a buyer from a homeowner –
he has no exploitation against him.

steps.triggerLanguage: הָהוּא גַּבְרָא דַּהֲוָה נָקֵיט וַרְשְׁכֵי לְזַבּוֹנֵי...

rashi.hebrew: מבעל הבית - תכשיטין וכלי תשמיש שלו חביבין עליו ואינו מוכרן אלא ביוקר והוה ליה כמפרש יודע אני שיש בו אונאה דאמרינן לקמן אין לו עליו אונאה כך מצאתי בשאילתות דרב אחאי (פ' בהר סי' קיג):
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that אונאה does not apply when buying from a בעל הבית because his items are precious to him and he only sells them at a high price, which is treated as if he explicitly stated he knows there is אונאה, citing שאלתות דרב אחאי.

steps.title: The parallel case of jewelry and רַב חִסְדָּא's ruling
steps.stepSummary: A parallel case involving jewelry is presented, where רַב חִסְדָּא gives the identical ruling that אונאה does not apply to a homeowner.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara presents a second, nearly identical case to reinforce the ruling. This is a setup line introducing a parallel example.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The structure mirrors the first case exactly: a seller announces sixty for an item worth fifty, would accept fifty-five. The buyer pays sixty to claim אונאה. רַב חִסְדָּא rules identically to רַבָּא: אונאה only applies to a merchant, not a homeowner. This repetition establishes that the ruling is not an isolated incident but a consistent legal principle.

keyTerms.meaning: the Amora ruling in the second case

phrases.aramaic: הָהוּא גַּבְרָא דַּהֲוָה נָקֵיט כִּיפֵּי לְזַבּוֹנֵי
phrases.english: That man who was holding jewelry to sell
Announced sixty and worth fifty,

phrases.aramaic: וְאִי הֲווֹ יָהֲבִי לֵיהּ חַמְשִׁין וְחַמְשָׁא הֲוָה שָׁקֵיל.
phrases.english: And if they would give him fifty and five he would take.
Came that man and said:

phrases.aramaic: אִי יָהֵיבְנָא לֵיהּ חַמְשִׁין וְחַמְשָׁא הָוְיָא מְחִילָה,
phrases.english: If I give him fifty and five I would waive,
I will give him sixty and I will sue him by law.

phrases.aramaic: אֲתָא לְקַמֵּיהּ דְּרַב חִסְדָּא. אֲמַר לֵיהּ:
phrases.english: Came before Rav Ḥisda. He said to him:

phrases.aramaic: לֹא שָׁנוּ אֶלָּא בְּלוֹקֵחַ מִן הַתַּגָּר,
phrases.english: They only taught regarding a buyer from a merchant,
But regarding a buyer from a homeowner –
He has no exploitation against him.

steps.triggerLanguage: הָהוּא גַּבְרָא דַּהֲוָה נָקֵיט כִּפֵּי לְזַבּוֹנֵי...

rashi.hebrew: הוי מחילה - לאלתר דהוה ליה פחות משתות:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the buyer's statement 'הוי מחילה' means he would immediately forgive the seller because the overcharge is less than a sixth, thus falling below the threshold for אונאה.

rashi.hebrew: ואתבעיה בדינא - בכדי שיראה לתגר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that 'ואתבעיה בדינא' means the buyer would take the seller to court to demonstrate that the transaction was conducted with a תגר, thereby establishing the legal precedent.

steps.title: Objection from the Mishna: אונאה applies to a layman
steps.stepSummary: רַב דִּימִי and רַבִּי אֶלְעָזָר affirm the ruling, but the Gemara objects from a Mishna that states אונאה applies equally to a layman and a merchant.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara raises a direct contradiction from the Mishna against רַב חִסְדָּא's ruling. It asks who the 'הֶדְיוֹט' in the Mishna is, assuming it means a regular homeowner.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב דִּימִי and רַבִּי אֶלְעָזָר initially agree with רַב חִסְדָּא. The Gemara challenges this by citing the Mishna: 'כְּשֵׁם שֶׁאוֹנָאָה לְהֶדְיוֹט כָּךְ אוֹנָאָה לַתַּגָּר'. If 'הֶדְיוֹט' means a homeowner, the Mishna explicitly teaches that אונאה applies to him, contradicting רַב חִסְדָּא.

keyTerms.meaning: the Amora who initially affirms the ruling

steps.whatToRemember: The Mishna seems to equate layman and merchant regarding אונאה, creating a direct conflict with the Amoraic rulings.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'הֶדְיוֹט' is the pivot point. The Gemara assumes it means a regular homeowner, which creates the contradiction.
steps.whyThisMatters: This is the central difficulty of the sugya: reconciling the Amoraic practice with the Tannaitic text.

phrases.english: Rav Dimi said to him: It is correct.
And likewise Rabbi Elazar said: It is correct.
And yet we learned:

phrases.aramaic: כְּשֵׁם שֶׁאוֹנָאָה לְהֶדְיוֹט כָּךְ אוֹנָאָה לַתַּגָּר.
phrases.english: Just as exploitation applies to a layman, so exploitation applies to a merchant.
Who is the layman? Is he not the homeowner?
Rav Ḥisda said: Regarding simple linen garments.
phrases.notes: Refers to the specific Mishnaic case of a homeowner selling items he made for sale.

phrases.english: But regarding vessels and garments of his personal use, which are precious to him,

phrases.aramaic: לָא מְזַבֵּין לְהוּ אִי לָאו בִּדְמֵי יַתִּירֵי.
phrases.english: he does not sell them unless for extra money.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב דִּימִי: יִשַּׁר. וְכֵן אָמַר רַבִּי אֶלְעָזָר: יִשַּׁר. וְהָא אֲנַן תַּנְיָן...

rashi.hebrew: בצדרייתא - בגדי קנבוס העומדים לימכר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that בצדרייתא refers to garments made of קנבוס (hemp) that are ready to be sold, which are less valuable items where the homeowner is not overly concerned about precise pricing.

steps.title: The Mishna: Ona'ah for buyer, seller, and merchants
steps.stepSummary: The Mishna establishes that Ona'ah applies to both buyer and seller, and to merchants, though R' Yehuda exempts merchants, and grants the exploited party the choice of annulment or compensation.
steps.whatsHappening: The Mishna is quoted, setting up the halachic parameters of Ona'ah. It teaches three points: buyer/seller, layman/merchant, and the exploited party's advantage.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Mishna teaches that Ona'ah applies to both buyer and seller. It applies to merchants (תַּגָּר) as well as laymen, though רַבִּי יְהוּדָה disagrees. The exploited party has the advantage (יָדוֹ עַל הָעֶלְיוֹנָה) and can choose to annul the sale or demand compensation.

keyTerms.meaning: he has the advantage / upper hand

steps.whatToRemember: The Mishna's structure: general rule (buyer/seller, layman/merchant), רַבִּי יְהוּדָה's dissent, and the remedy (choice of annulment or compensation).
steps.confusionAlert: Note that רַבִּי יְהוּדָה's dissent is about merchants, not about buyer vs seller.
steps.whyThisMatters: This Mishna is the foundation for the entire sugya on Ona'ah, particularly the status of merchants and the rights of the exploited party.

phrases.aramaic: מַתְנִי׳ אֶחָד הַלּוֹקֵחַ וְאֶחָד הַמּוֹכֵר יֵשׁ לָהֶן אוֹנָאָה.
phrases.english: MISHNA: Both the buyer and the seller are subject to the halakhot of exploitation.

phrases.aramaic: כְּשֵׁם שֶׁאוֹנָאָה לַהֶדְיוֹט כָּךְ אוֹנָאָה לַתַּגָּר.
phrases.english: Just as the halakhot of exploitation apply to a layman, so do the halakhot of…

phrases.aramaic: רַבִּי יְהוּדָה אוֹמֵר: אֵין אוֹנָאָה לַתַּגָּר.
phrases.english: Rabbi Yehuda says: There is no exploitation for a merchant.
The one upon whom the exploitation was imposed
has the advantage.
If he wishes, he can say to the other:
Give me back my money
or he can say: Give me back the sum that you gained by exploiting me.

steps.triggerLanguage: מַתְנִי׳ אֶחָד הַלּוֹקֵחַ וְאֶחָד הַמּוֹכֵר יֵשׁ לָהֶן אוֹנָאָה.

rashi.hebrew: מתני' ר' יהודה אומר אין אונאה לתגר - לקמיה מפרש לה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י notes that the Mishna's statement of רבי יהודה's dissent regarding אונאה for a תגר will be explained later in the Gemara.

rashi.hebrew: מי שהוטל עליו - מי שנתאנה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the phrase מי שהוטל עליו refers to the party who was exploited (נתאנה) by the other.

rashi.hebrew: תן לי מעותי - אם נתאנה לוקח:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the option to demand תן לי מעותי applies specifically when the buyer is the one who was exploited.

tosafot.hebrew: והתנן כשם שאונאה להדיוט כך אונאה לתגר - פירוש לאפוקי מר' יהודה דאמר תגר אין לו אונאה כשמכר בזול לפי שהוא בקי ואחולי אחיל ליה והאי דזבני' הכי משום דאתרמי ליה זבינתא אחריתי כדאמרי' בסמוך והשתא פריך שפיר דע"כ מיירי בהדיוט מוכר מדתלי תגר בהדיוט דטעמא דידע ומחיל לא שייך אלא במוכר וא"ת ומנ"ל דמיירי בהדיוט שמוכר ביוקר ונתאנה לוקח דילמא מיירי בהדיוט שמכר בזול ונתאנה דהשתא הוי דומיא דתגר וי"ל דאונאה משמע בכל ענין בין שהוא מאנה בין שהוא מתאנה:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the Mishna's comparison of a תגר to a הדיוט is specifically to exclude רבי יהודה's opinion that a merchant who sells cheaply has no אונאה because he is knowledgeable and waived his rights, and therefore the Mishna must be discussing a layman seller to establish that אונאה applies generally regardless of who is exploited.

steps.title: From where do we derive Ona'ah for both buyer and seller?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks for the scriptural source for Ona'ah applying to both buyer and seller, citing a Baraita that derives it from Leviticus 25:14.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara asks a neutral question about the source of the Mishna's first clause. It cites a Baraita using the verse 'וְכִי תִמְכְּרוּ מִמְכָּר אוֹ קָנֹה' to derive Ona'ah for both.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara asks 'מִנְּהָנֵי מִלֵּי?' — from where do we derive this? It cites a Baraita that uses 'וְכִי תִמְכְּרוּ מִמְכָּר' to derive Ona'ah for the buyer, and 'אוֹ קָנֹה' to derive it for the seller.
steps.whatToRemember: The verse has two parts: 'מִמְכָּר' (selling) and 'אוֹ קָנֹה' (buying). The Baraita uses 'מִמְכָּר' for the buyer and 'אוֹ קָנֹה' for the seller.
steps.confusionAlert: Note the counter-intuitive derivation: 'מִמְכָּר' (selling) is used for the BUYER, and 'אוֹ קָנֹה' (buying) is used for the SELLER. This is because the verse says 'if you sell... or buy... do not exploit'. The Baraita reads 'if you sell [to him]' as the context for the buyer being exploited, and 'or buy [from him]' as the context for the seller being exploited.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the need for both parts of the verse, which the Gemara will explain in the next step.

phrases.english: GEMARA: From where are these matters derived?
As the Sages taught: "And if you sell to your colleague an item that is sold,
you shall not exploit."
I have only derived a case where the buyer was exploited,
from where do I derive [that] the seller was exploited?
The Torah states: "Or acquire
you shall not exploit."

steps.title: Why both buyer and seller are needed in the verse
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains why both 'buyer' and 'seller' are written in the verse: each could have been excluded based on their respective roles in the transaction.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara provides a צריכותא (necessity) explanation for the doubled wording in the verse. It explains why both 'seller' and 'buyer' are explicitly mentioned.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara explains that if only 'seller' were written, we might think Ona'ah only applies to him because he knows the value of his goods, but the buyer doesn't know, so maybe he's not warned. If only 'buyer' were written, we might think Ona'ah only applies to him because he acquires something, but the seller loses something, so maybe he's not warned. Therefore, both are needed.

keyTerms.meaning: he is certain of his merchandise's value

steps.whatToRemember: The logic is: seller knows value → buyer doesn't. Buyer acquires → seller loses. Each could be excluded based on their unique position, so both are explicitly warned.
steps.confusionAlert: Note the asymmetry: the seller is warned because he knows the value, the buyer is warned because he acquires. The Gemara is explaining why the Torah couldn't just warn one and let the other be derived.
steps.whyThisMatters: This is a classic צריכותא structure, showing how the Gemara reads the Torah as precise and non-redundant.

phrases.aramaic: וְאִיצְטְרִיךְ לְמִכְתַּב לוֹקֵחַ וְאִיצְטְרִיךְ לְמִכְתַּב מוֹכֵר,
phrases.english: And it is necessary to write buyer, and it is necessary to write seller,
For if the Merciful One wrote seller –
Because he is certain of his sale,
But buyer –
Who is not certain of his sale,

phrases.aramaic: אֵימָא לָא אַזְהֲרֵיהּ רַחֲמָנָא בְּ״לֹא תּוֹנוּ״.
phrases.english: Say that the Merciful One did not warn him in 'You shall not exploit.'
And if the Merciful One wrote buyer –
Because he is acquiring,
For people say: 'You purchased, you acquired.'
But seller who is losing,
For people say: 'One who sells, loses,'

phrases.aramaic: אֵימָא לָא אַזְהֲרֵיהּ רַחֲמָנָא בְּ״לֹא תּוֹנוּ״, צְרִיכָא.
phrases.english: Say that the Merciful One did not warn him in 'You shall not exploit.' It is necessary.

steps.triggerLanguage: וְאִיצְטְרִיךְ לְמִכְתַּב לוֹקֵחַ וְאִיצְטְרִיךְ לְמִכְתַּב מוֹכֵר

rashi.hebrew: גמ' דאי כתב רחמנא מוכר - שהוא מוזהר בבל תונו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the verse's mention of the seller establishes that he is explicitly warned against אונאה.

rashi.hebrew: דקים ליה בזבינתיה - כמה נתן בה ובמזיד הוא עושה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the seller is considered to know the value of his goods (קים ליה בזבינתיה) and thus acts with intent when engaging in the transaction.

rashi.hebrew: זבנת קנית - אם לקחת חפץ המתקיים שכר הוא אצלך שלא הוצאת המעות ביציאה והרי הוא מתקיים לך:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י interprets the phrase זבנת קנית to mean that the buyer gains a lasting benefit since the money he spent remains with him in the form of the acquired item.

rashi.hebrew: זבין אוביד - מכרת חפץ שלך הרי אתה נפסד שיכלו המעות ביציאה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י interprets the phrase זבין אוביד to mean that the seller suffers a loss because his money leaves his possession upon the sale.

steps.title: Challenge to R' Yehuda: How can a merchant be exempt from Ona'ah?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges R' Yehuda's statement that there is no Ona'ah for a merchant, questioning how expertise could exempt one from a Torah prohibition.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara attacks רַבִּי יְהוּדָה's dissent in the Mishna. It expresses surprise that a merchant could be exempt from Ona'ah just because of his profession.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara asks: 'מִשּׁוּם שֶׁהוּא תַּגָּר אֵין לוֹ אוֹנָאָה?!' — just because he's a merchant, he's exempt from Ona'ah? The implication is that anyone could make a mistake in valuation, so expertise shouldn't matter.

keyTerms.meaning: there is no Ona'ah for a merchant

steps.whatToRemember: The challenge is based on the principle that Ona'ah is a Torah prohibition, and expertise shouldn't exempt one from it.
steps.confusionAlert: Note that the Gemara is not yet challenging the logic of רַבִּי יְהוּדָה, but rather the very idea that a merchant could be exempt.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the need for a resolution that explains how a merchant could be exempt without violating the Torah prohibition.

phrases.aramaic: רַבִּי יְהוּדָה אוֹמֵר: אֵין אוֹנָאָה לַתַּגָּר.
phrases.english: Rabbi Yehuda says: There is no exploitation for a merchant.

phrases.aramaic: מִשּׁוּם שֶׁהוּא תַּגָּר אֵין לוֹ אוֹנָאָה? !
phrases.english: Because he is a merchant, he has no exploitation?

steps.triggerLanguage: רַבִּי יְהוּדָה אוֹמֵר: אֵין אוֹנָאָה לַתַּגָּר. מִשּׁוּם שֶׁהוּא תַּגָּר אֵין לוֹ אוֹנָאָה?!

steps.title: Rav Nachman: The merchant waives Ona'ah
steps.stepSummary: Rav Nachman explains that R' Yehuda is talking about a professional trader who knows the value and waives the Ona'ah, selling at a loss only to free up capital for new merchandise.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב נַחְמָן provides a resolution to the challenge against רַבִּי יְהוּדָה. He says that רַבִּי יְהוּדָה is referring to a 'תַּגָּר סַפְסָר' — a professional trader.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב נַחְמָן says that רַבִּי יְהוּדָה is referring to a 'תַּגָּר סַפְסָר' — a professional trader. Such a person knows the value of his goods and waives the Ona'ah. He sells at a loss only because new merchandise has become available and he needs the cash to buy it. Thus, he is not really being exploited; he is choosing to sell at a lower price for business reasons.

keyTerms.meaning: Rav Nachman says that Rav says
he knows the value of his merchandise
he waives the sum for himself

steps.whatToRemember: The key is that the merchant waives the Ona'ah. He is not being exploited; he is making a business decision.
steps.confusionAlert: Note that this resolution changes the nature of the transaction: it's not a case of Ona'ah at all, but a voluntary sale at a lower price.
steps.whyThisMatters: This introduces the concept of 'waiving Ona'ah', which is crucial for understanding the status of merchants in commercial law.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַב נַחְמָן אָמַר רַב: בְּתַגָּר סַפְסָר שָׁנוּ.
phrases.english: Rav Naḥman says Rav says: With a merchant, a trader, they taught.
What is the reason?
From knowing he knew his merchandise how much it is worth,
and he waived and waived upon him.
And this that he sold it thus –
because of that other merchandise happened to him.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַב נַחְמָן אָמַר רַב: בְּתַגָּר סַפְסָר שָׁנוּ.

rashi.hebrew: ספסר - קונה ומוכר מיד תמיד:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines a ספסר as a professional merchant who continuously buys and sells goods immediately.

rashi.hebrew: מידע ידע זבינתיה כמה שויא - הרי לא שהה בין קנייתה למכירתה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the merchant knows the true value of his merchandise because he does not delay between purchasing and selling it.

steps.title: Challenge: But he retracts from the transaction!
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges Rav Nachman's resolution: if the merchant can retract from the transaction, then he didn't really waive the Ona'ah.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara attacks רַב נַחְמָן's resolution. It asks: 'וְהַשְׁתָּא מִיהָא קָא הָדַר בֵּיהּ!' — but now, in any case, he retracts from the transaction!
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara asks: 'וְהַשְׁתָּא מִיהָא קָא הָדַר בֵּיהּ!' — but now, in any case, he retracts from the transaction! If he can retract, then he didn't really waive the Ona'ah; he was still exploited and is now seeking to undo the sale.
steps.whatToRemember: The challenge is based on the fact that the merchant has the right to retract, which contradicts the idea that he waived the Ona'ah.
steps.confusionAlert: Note that this challenge assumes that the merchant has the right to retract, which is a key point in the sugya.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the need for a further resolution that explains how the merchant can retract without contradicting the idea that he waived the Ona'ah.

phrases.english: And now, in any case, he is retracting from it!
Rav Ashi said:
What is the meaning of: there is no ona'ah for a merchant –
He is not subject to the law of ona'ah,
Even if it is less than the measure of ona'ah, he returns.
It is taught in a baraita in accordance with Rav Naḥman:
Rabbi Yehuda says:
A merchant has no ona'ah,
Because he is expert.

rashi.hebrew: שאפי' פחות מכדי אונאה חוזר - אם נתאנה דחייו תלויין בכך ותקנו לו שיחזור:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's concern about retraction applies even when the overcharge is less than the threshold of אונאה, because the merchant's livelihood depends on the transaction and a decree was made to allow him to retract.

rashi.hebrew: תניא כוותיה דרב נחמן - דאפי' בכדי אונאה אינו חוזר בו ואין מחזירין לו אונאה מדתלי טעמא מפני שהוא בקי כגון ספסר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the ברייתא supports רב נחמן's view that a merchant cannot claim אונאה even when the overcharge meets the threshold, because the reason given is that he is an expert (בקי), such as a ספסר.

steps.title: Whose opinion is the Mishna?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks whose opinion is expressed in the Mishna's statement that the exploited party has the advantage and can choose annulment or compensation.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara asks a neutral question about the attribution of the Mishna's final clause. It asks: 'מַנִּי מַתְנִיתִין?' — whose opinion is the Mishna?
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara asks: 'מַנִּי מַתְנִיתִין?' — whose opinion is the Mishna? It then states that it is neither רַבִּי נָתָן nor רַבִּי יְהוּדָה הַנָּשִׂיא, setting up a difficulty.

keyTerms.term: לָא רַבִּי נָתָן וְלָא רַבִּי יְהוּדָה הַנָּשִׂיא
keyTerms.meaning: neither R' Natan nor R' Yehuda HaNasi

steps.whatToRemember: The question is about the attribution of the Mishna's final clause, which grants the exploited party the choice of annulment or compensation.
steps.confusionAlert: Note that the Gemara is not yet challenging the Mishna, but asking for its attribution.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up a difficulty that will be resolved by several Amoraim.

phrases.aramaic: מִי שֶׁהוּטַּל עָלָיו יָדוֹ עַל הָעֶלְיוֹנָה וְכוּ׳.
phrases.english: The one upon whom the hand was placed on the upper one, etc.
Whose is our Mishna?

phrases.aramaic: לָא רַבִּי נָתָן וְלָא רַבִּי יְהוּדָה הַנָּשִׂיא.
phrases.english: Neither Rabbi Natan nor Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi.

steps.triggerLanguage: מִי שֶׁהוּטַּל עָלָיו יָדוֹ עַל הָעֶלְיוֹנָה וְכוּ׳. מַנִּי מַתְנִיתִין? לָא רַבִּי נָתָן וְלָא רַבִּי יְהוּדָה הַנָּשִׂיא.

steps.title: Why it's not רַבִּי נָתָן or רַבִּי יְהוּדָה הַנָּשִׂיא
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains why the Mishna cannot be attributed to R' Natan or R' Yehuda HaNasi, based on differences in their respective Baraitot.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא explains the difficulty raised in the previous step. It shows why the מִשְׁנָה doesn't match either תַּנָּא.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא notes a textual mismatch. If it were רַבִּי נָתָן, the מִשְׁנָה says 'רָצָה' but the בְּרִיתָא doesn't. If it were רַבִּי יְהוּדָה הַנָּשִׂיא, the מִשְׁנָה says 'לוֹקֵחַ' but the בְּרִיתָא says 'מוֹכֵר'.

keyTerms.meaning: the בְּרִיתָא does not teach 'if he wishes'
if it is רַבִּי יְהוּדָה הַנָּשִׂיא

phrases.english: If Rabbi Natan –
Our Mishnah teaches "if he wishes",
and the Baraita does not teach "if he wishes".
If Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi –
Our Mishnah teaches "buyer",
the Baraita teaches "seller".

steps.triggerLanguage: אִי רַבִּי נָתָן – מַתְנִיתִין קָתָנֵי ״רָצָה״, וּבָרַיְיתָא לָא קָתָנֵי ״רָצָה״. אִי רַבִּי יְהוּדָה הַנָּשִׂיא – מַתְנִיתִין קָתָנֵי ״לוֹקֵחַ״, בָּרַיְיתָא קָתָנֵי ״מוֹכֵר״.

rashi.hebrew: קתני רצה - דידו על העליונה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Mishna's use of 'רצה' indicates the buyer has the choice to keep the item at the higher price.

rashi.hebrew: לא קתני רצה - אלא על כרחו קנה ומחזיר לו אונאה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that R' Natan's Baraita omits 'רצה' because the buyer acquires the item involuntarily and must return the overcharge.

rashi.hebrew: מתני' קתני לוקח - היכא דנתאנה לוקח קתני דידו על העליונה כדקתני תן לי מעותי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י notes that the Mishna's reference to 'לוקח' applies when the buyer was overcharged, giving him the right to keep the item at the higher price.

rashi.hebrew: קתני מוכר - דהיכא דנתאנה מוכר הוא דידו על העליונה אבל אם נתאנה לוקח לא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that R' Yehuda HaNasi's Baraita uses 'מוכר' because only the seller benefits from the higher price when the seller was overcharged, unlike when the buyer was overcharged.

steps.title: Mnemonic for the אַמּוֹרָאִים who resolve the difficulty
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara provides a mnemonic (סימן) for the four Amoraim who will resolve the difficulty: R' Elazar, Rabba, Rava, and Rav Ashi.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא introduces a mnemonic device for the upcoming resolutions. It prepares the reader for four different opinions.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא gives a mnemonic: 'זב רש'. This stands for זַיִן (רַבִּי אֶלְעָזָר), בֵּית (רַבָּא), רֵישׁ (רַבָּא), שִׁין (רַב אַשֵּׁי). This organizes the four resolutions that follow.

phrases.english: (Sign: Zayin, Beit, Reish, Shin)

steps.title: רַבִּי אֶלְעָזָר: I don't know who taught it
steps.stepSummary: R' Elazar states that he doesn't know who taught the Mishna, as it doesn't match any known opinion.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבִּי אֶלְעָזָר gives his opinion on the attribution of the מִשְׁנָה. He states he doesn't know the source.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּי אֶלְעָזָר says: 'אוֹנָאָה זוֹ אֵינִי יוֹדֵעַ מִי שְׁנָאָהּ'. He implies the מִשְׁנָה doesn't match any known תַּנָּא. This acknowledges the difficulty without resolving it.

keyTerms.term: אוֹנָאָה זוֹ אֵינִי יוֹדֵעַ מִי שְׁנָאָהּ
keyTerms.meaning: I don't know who taught this אוֹנָאָה

phrases.english: Rabbi Elazar says:

phrases.aramaic: אוֹנָאָה זוֹ אֵינִי יוֹדֵעַ מִי שְׁנָאָהּ.
phrases.english: Concerning this halakha of exploitation, I do not know who taught it.

phrases.aramaic: רַבָּה אָמַר: לְעוֹלָם רַבִּי נָתָן הִיא,
phrases.english: Rabba said: Actually, it is the opinion of Rabbi Natan,
and emend and teach in the baraita as well: 'If he wishes.' Rava said:
Actually, it is the opinion of Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi,

phrases.aramaic: וּמַאי דְּשַׁיַּיר בְּמַתְנִיתִין קָא מְפָרֵישׁ בְּבָרַיְיתָא.
phrases.english: and what is omitted in the mishna he explicates in the baraita.
Rav Ashi said: The language is also precise,
as the tanna teaches: 'Both the buyer and the seller,'
and he elucidates it for the buyer,

phrases.aramaic: שְׁמַע מִינַּהּ שַׁיּוֹרֵיה שַׁיְּירֵיהּ לְמוֹכֵר. שְׁמַע מִינַּהּ.
phrases.english: learn from it that he omitted the halakha of the seller. Learn from it.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַבִּי אֶלְעָזָר: אוֹנָאָה זוֹ אֵינִי יוֹדֵעַ מִי שְׁנָאָהּ.

rashi.hebrew: אונאה זו - משנתינו זו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that ר' אלעזר's statement 'אונאה זו' refers specifically to the Mishna under discussion.

rashi.hebrew: ותני נמי בברייתא - בר' נתן רצה ובלוקח ומוכר הוא דפליגי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבה's suggestion that the Mishna follows ר' נתן is supported by a ברייתא which states that ר' נתן holds 'רצה' (the buyer's consent is sufficient) and that the dispute between the buyer and seller is the central issue.

rashi.hebrew: דיקא נמי - דמתניתין במוכר נמי ס"ל הכי ומפרש ללוקח ולא פירש למוכר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains רב אשי's proof that the Mishna applies to the seller as well, noting that the Mishna explicitly explains the law for the buyer but omits an explanation for the seller, implying the seller's law is identical and thus requires no separate elaboration.

steps.title: The dispute between רַב and שְׁמוּאֵל on waiving אוֹנָאָה
steps.stepSummary: The sugya opens with an איתמר presenting the dispute between Rav and Shmuel regarding the validity of a stipulation waiving Ona'ah.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with an אִיתְּמַר presenting the dispute between רַב and שְׁמוּאֵל. They disagree on the validity of a stipulation waiving אוֹנָאָה.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב holds the stipulation is invalid. שְׁמוּאֵל holds the stipulation is valid. The גמרא suggests aligning them with the תַּנָּאִים dispute of רַבִּי מֵאִיר and רַבִּי יְהוּדָה.

keyTerms.meaning: it was stated [introducing an אַמּוֹרָאִים dispute]

phrases.english: It was stated:

phrases.aramaic: הָאוֹמֵר לַחֲבֵירוֹ עַל מְנָת שֶׁאֵין לְךָ עָלַי אוֹנָאָה.
phrases.english: One who says to his friend: on condition that you have no exploitation against me.

phrases.aramaic: רַב אָמַר: יֵשׁ לוֹ עָלָיו אוֹנָאָה.
phrases.english: Rav says: He has exploitation against him.

phrases.aramaic: וּשְׁמוּאֵל אָמַר: אֵין לוֹ עָלָיו אוֹנָאָה.
phrases.english: And Shmuel says: He does not have exploitation against him.
Let us say Rav who says like Rabbi Meir,
And Shmuel who says like Rabbi Yehuda.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִיתְּמַר: הָאוֹמֵר לַחֲבֵירוֹ עַל מְנָת שֶׁאֵין לְךָ עָלַי אוֹנָאָה. רַב אָמַר: יֵשׁ לוֹ עָלָיו אוֹנָאָה. וּשְׁמוּאֵל אָמַר: אֵין לוֹ עָלָיו אוֹנָאָה. לֵימָא רַב דְּאָמַר כְּרַבִּי מֵאִיר, וּשְׁמוּאֵל דְּאָמַר כְּרַבִּי יְהוּדָה.

rashi.hebrew: לימא רב דאמר כר' מאיר - דאמר מתנה על מה שכתוב בתורה תנאו בטל אפילו בדבר שבממון:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רב follows רבי מאיר, who holds that a stipulation overriding a Torah law is invalid even in monetary matters.

steps.title: The בְּרִיתָא of רַבִּי מֵאִיר and רַבִּי יְהוּדָה on conditions
steps.stepSummary: A Baraita is cited to support the alignment of Rav with R' Meir and Shmuel with R' Yehuda regarding the validity of conditions in monetary matters.
steps.whatsHappening: A בְּרִיתָא is cited to support the alignment of רַב with רַבִּי מֵאִיר and שְׁמוּאֵל with רַבִּי יְהוּדָה. It discusses conditions in monetary matters.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The בְּרִיתָא discusses a condition waiving שֵׁאֵר כְּסוּת וְעוֹנָה. רַבִּי מֵאִיר says the condition is void. רַבִּי יְהוּדָה says in monetary matters the condition stands. This aligns רַב with רַבִּי מֵאִיר and שְׁמוּאֵל with רַבִּי יְהוּדָה.

phrases.aramaic: דְּתַנְיָא: הָאוֹמֵר לְאִשָּׁה הֲרֵי אַתְּ מְקוּדֶּשֶׁת לִי
phrases.english: As it is taught: One who says to a woman: Behold you are betrothed to me

phrases.aramaic: עַל מְנָת שֶׁאֵין לְךָ עָלַי שְׁאֵר
phrases.english: on condition that you have no claim against me
for clothing and conjugal rights –
Behold she is betrothed, and his condition is void,
the words of Rabbi Meir.

phrases.aramaic: רַבִּי יְהוּדָה אוֹמֵר: בְּדָבָר שֶׁבְּמָמוֹן – תְּנָאוֹ קַיָּים.
phrases.english: Rabbi Yehuda says: In a matter of money – his condition is valid.

steps.triggerLanguage: דְּתַנְיָא: הָאוֹמֵר לְאִשָּׁה הֲרֵי אַתְּ מְקוּדֶּשֶׁת לִי עַל מְנָת שֶׁאֵין לְךָ עָלַי שְׁאֵר כְּסוּת וְעוֹנָה – הֲרֵי זוֹ מְקוּדֶּשֶׁת, וּתְנָאוֹ בָּטֵל, דִּבְרֵי רַבִּי מֵאִיר. רַבִּי יְהוּדָה אוֹמֵר: בְּדָבָר שֶׁבְּמָמוֹן – תְּנָאוֹ קַיָּים.

rashi.hebrew: ר' יהודה אומר בדבר שבממון - כגון שאר וכסות ניתן למחילה והרי מחלה אבל עונה שהיא צער הגוף לא ניתן למחילה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבי יהודה's ruling that the condition stands in monetary matters applies to שֵׁאֵר and כְּסוּת, which can be waived, but not to עוֹנָה, which involves bodily pain and cannot be waived.

steps.title: רַב's defense: Even according to רַבִּי יְהוּדָה, רַב is correct
steps.stepSummary: Rav defends his position by distinguishing between a known waiver of rights and an unknown potential Ona'ah.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב defends his position by distinguishing between a known waiver and an unknown potential אוֹנָאָה. He argues his ruling stands even according to רַבִּי יְהוּדָה.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב argues רַבִּי יְהוּדָה only validates conditions where the party KNOWS they are waiving a right. In אוֹנָאָה, the buyer doesn't know he's being exploited. He can't waive a right he doesn't know exists.

keyTerms.meaning: he knew and is waiving [his right]
who knows that he is waiving?

phrases.english: Rav said to you:
I, even according to Rabbi Yehuda –

phrases.aramaic: עַד כָּאן לָא קָאָמַר רַבִּי יְהוּדָה הָתָם,
phrases.english: Until here Rabbi Yehuda does not say there,
But that she knew and waived,
But here, who knows that he waives?

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר לְךָ רַב: אֲנָא דַּאֲמַרִי אֲפִילּוּ לְרַבִּי יְהוּדָה – עַד כָּאן לָא קָאָמַר רַבִּי יְהוּדָה הָתָם, אֶלָּא דְּיָדְעָה וְקָא מָחֲלָה, אֲבָל הָכָא מִי יָדַע דְּמָחֵיל?
